Understanding Rust Ownership

Rust's ownership system is the foundation of its memory safety guarantees. Unlike garbage-collected languages, Rust enforces memory management at compile time.

The Three Rules of Ownership

  • Each value in Rust has an owner
  • There can only be one owner at a time
  • When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped
fn main() {
    let s = String::from("hello");
    let s2 = s; // s is moved into s2
    // println!("{}", s); // ERROR: s is no longer valid
    println!("{}", s2); // OK
}

After the move, s is no longer valid. This prevents use-after-free bugs.

Borrowing

References allow you to use a value without taking ownership:

fn calculate_length(s: &String) -> usize {
    s.len()
}

fn main() {
    let s1 = String::from("hello");
    let len = calculate_length(&s1);
    println!("Length of '{}' is {}", s1, len);
}
